role_summary: Dentist; adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine, Florida; president of the SCLC Florida branch; survivor of a September 1963 KKK kidnapping and beating; invited Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964, the campaign that helped produce the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
ai_reading: Hayling's top semantic neighbor is C. T. Vivian (entry 134) at a high cosine count of 220, more than double his next neighbor; the catalog's clearest SCLC-direct-action pairing outside the Wyatt Tee Walker / Vivian dyad. The pairing fits the cultural record (Hayling presided over Florida SCLC and brought King into the St. Augustine campaign that drew Vivian and other SCLC leadership) but the embedding count is notably high for an interviewee whose own cultural placement is regional and dental-professional rather than SCLC-headquarters. His other neighbors are the joint Hamilton/Ulmer interview (entry 4), Robert L. Carter (entry 108, NAACP LDF), Gwendolyn Annette Perkins Duncan (entry 51), and H. Jack Geiger (entry 54, Mississippi community health). His axis positions are extraordinarily centrist: all five are within +/-0.18 of zero, matching the same vector-centrism pattern visible in McDew (entry 16), Terry (entry 40), and Richardson (entry 49). For a survivor of a documented KKK kidnapping and beating, the embedding's near-zero placement across all axes is the novel observation: the violent specificity of the September 1963 incident does not pull his testimony toward any pole, possibly catching the dentist's clinical-professional register more than the survivor's pain-narrative register.
biographical_paragraph: Hayling was the first African American dentist elected to the local, regional, and national components of the American Dental Association [src: 2]. As adult adviser to the NAACP Youth Council in St. Augustine he led protests against segregation during the city's 400th-anniversary commemorations of 1965 [src: 2]. In September 1963 he and three companions (James Jackson, Clyde Jenkins, and James Hauser) were kidnapped and taken to a Ku Klux Klan rally, where they were severely beaten [src: 2]. He served as president of the Florida branch of the Southern Christian Leadership Conference and invited Martin Luther King Jr. to St. Augustine in spring 1964; the campaign that followed was pivotal for the passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964 [src: 2]. He was inducted into the Florida Civil Rights Hall of Fame in 2014; St. Augustine's largest park has since been named in his honor [src: 2]. Born November 20, 1929 in Tallahassee, Florida, Hayling died December 20, 2015 in Fort Lauderdale at eighty-six [src: 2]. His Library of Congress oral history is entry 103 of the Civil Rights History Project (American Folklife Center accession AFC 2010/039) [src: 1].

sources.title: Robert Bagner Hayling oral history
